title: Lower Lift
desc: Castle Pillar's lower gallery still needs supplies, even if nobody enjoys talking about it.

steps.text:
There are levels in Castle Pillar that ordinary readers never see and workers discuss only when necessary. The lower gallery is one of them. Some say it stores dangerous records. Some say it once housed prisoners. Some say worse and pretend not to mean it. What matters for daily life is simpler: a lift still runs there, and lifts do not remain trustworthy by accident.

This morning the platform needs its regular service before the next sealed crate goes down. Counterweight sand has settled unevenly in one bucket line, and the chain on the guide side wants inspection before anyone lets a full load hang over that shaft.

The lift tender rests a lantern on the stone and shrugs at your expression. "It is only machinery," he says. Then, after a beat: "Mostly." You can rebalance the counterweight buckets, or examine the chain and test bell for the guide side.

options.label: Inspect the chain and test the guide bell

steps.text:
The counterweight line runs through a narrow chamber beside the shaft, each bucket packed with sand that can be scooped, shifted, and measured against a marked rod. The work is tedious and heavy, which is exactly what makes it reassuring. Whatever the lower gallery is, the lift serves it by rules you can weigh in both hands.

You add from one bucket, remove from another, and watch the balance mark creep back toward center while the tender checks the platform response above. When the final measure is right, the lift settles into a smooth patient hover instead of its earlier sideways complaint.

The tender presses his palm to the stone beside the shaft and listens for a moment before nodding. "Good. Now the platform remembers how to behave." He does not invite you to ask what it was doing before. You do not offer.

options.label: Seal the sand bins and close the service hatch

steps.text:
The guide chain descends into a darkness that the lantern barely bruises. Link by link, you inspect it for wear while the tender stands at the bell line above. Every few lengths he rings once, and the note travels down the shaft in a strange clean fall that seems to keep going longer than it should.

Most of the chain is fine: oiled, cold, obedient. One section near the service stop has begun to shed rust powder where the rollers meet. You clean it, apply fresh oil, and test the motion until the roughness smooths. Then the tender rings the bell again. This time the tone comes back without a hitch in it.

"Good enough for another day," he says. In Castle Pillar, that is high praise for anything connected to the lower levels. You hang the inspection hook back on the wall and notice that you are breathing more quietly than when you arrived.

options.label: Step away from the shaft and log the work
